content.full_text:
$sr i
, -- ,., t$? 
17 october 1992
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street
Sacramento, California 
95814
Attn: Us. Carla Wardlow, I\,tgr. Technical Assistance Division
Dear Ms. wardlow:
I am the Treasurer for a city council candidate in Santa Ana and I am
atso the creator of the TIN CUP ordinance in Orange County. I have
been monitoring campaign contributions for the past 18 years to assure
to the best of my ability that the Political Reform Act and the Iocal
TIN CUP Campaign Reform Ordinance (enacted Dec. 1978) are complied
with.
I have some concerns over the lack of information being reported in
the Form 490 regarding campaign expenditures. Up until recently
most candidates offered a "brief" two or three-r,/ord statement to
describe their expenses. However, the FPPC staff is apparently advising
candidates/Treasurers that it is sufficient to merely designate the
expenditure by a code letter.
f would like to take issue with this advice and I specifically wish to
call your attention to Section 8421I(j)4, which requires a "brief"
description of the expenditure, but does not in any way suggest that a
mere code letter (which by the way is nowhere defined on the campaign
di.sclosure 490 forms) is sufficient. 
If the purpose of the Act is to
fu1ly and truthfully disclose contributions and expenditures in order
that the voters be fully informed (section 81002), then I submit that
the use of a code letter alone is woefully inadequate.
As an example of my concerns, I have included a complete copy of Supvsr.
wieder's most recent 490 Forn. Schedule E on pps.7 -22 list her expen-
ditures by code letter only. There is absolutely no way to judge whether
these are legitinate potitical expenses because the code letters alone
do not define the exPenditure. Secondly, as I said above, the public
does not know what the code letters mean since they also are not defined
anywhere on the form.
specifically, look at the reimbursements made to Harriett wi-eder on page
7 ($375 and $47), page 13 ($260 and 5562), and page 18 ($708) --- all
using the code letlei C. Similarly, reimbursenents made to her husband
Irv frieder (pages 8, I0 and 2I totalling $293) are also reported simply
as c expensei. Do you believe this paltry information meets the dis-
closure purposes of the Act?
Another example -- anyone reading these forms r^rould be at a complete
loss to figuie out hrh;t expenses were incurred with "The Trusteeship"
reported on pages 11, 16 and 21. And on paqe 22, what expenses were
in;urred wittr llanar. and cEwAER when it"isn't 
apparent what type of
business these entities are engaged inl
I


Paqe 2
of course some of the expenses are readily determined by virtue of the
payee's name - i.e., Pacific Bell and GTE are obviously phone bi1Is, the
U.S. Post Office is obviously for postage. In these cases I aqree that the
code letter is sufficient since one can tel1 by the payee what the ex-
penses are.
In addition to the above concerns, I would like to call your attention to
some expenses apparently connected with travel (made to stellar Travel
on pp. 9 and 1,6, and possibly E.D.L. Salute to Anaheim on page 14).
oo lhese items conform to the requirements of section 8421r(j)7 0f the Act?
It has been suggested to me by a member of your staff that I request an
investigation by your office to determine whether the expenses reported
on the enclosed form are legitimate political expenditures. I find this
to be a potential waste of your time and our money and could be avoided
completely if a brief description of the expenditures were provided by
the filing candidate.
I woufd like to request that you review the enclosed campaign statement
to deternine whether it adequately meets the reguirements of the Act spe-
cifically with regard to the reportinq of expenses. Secondly, I am also
asking you to reconsider your advice that the use of code letters alone
meets-t[e requirements of 84211 (j)4. 
Thirdly, it would be advisable to
at least inciude the definition of the code letters on the first 
page of
Schedule E.
I will anxiously await a response to these issues.
Sincerely,
*/-%{*J,*L
Shirley L. Grindle
Chairman TIN CUP Campaign Reform Committee
Treasurer, Com[ittee to Elect Miguet Pulido
*19051 Glen Arran
orange, Ca1if. 9266 9
Phone: (714) 633-0851
encl.
z

sections.question_synthetic: The requester challenges FPPC staff advice that campaign expenditures may be reported using only code letters on Form 490, arguing that Section 84211(j)4 requires a 'brief' description and that code letters alone do not adequately disclose expenditure information to voters as required by Section 81002.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is an incoming request/complaint letter, not an FPPC advice letter response. The document contains no formal QUESTION or CONCLUSION sections. The writer is requesting FPPC action/review rather than seeking advice. Includes specific examples of disputed expenditure entries and requests three specific actions from FPPC.

embedding.qa_text: QUESTION: The requester challenges FPPC staff advice that campaign expenditures may be reported using only code letters on Form 490, arguing that Section 84211(j)4 requires a 'brief' description and that code letters alone do not adequately disclose expenditure information to voters as required by Section 81002.

embedding.summary: A city council candidate's treasurer and creator of the TIN CUP ordinance submits a complaint to the FPPC challenging the adequacy of code-letter-only expenditure reporting on Form 490 Schedule E, arguing it violates disclosure requirements and requesting the FPPC review a specific candidate's campaign statement and reconsider its advice on code letter sufficiency.
